title: How to Crack the Quantitative Section of Sukkur IBA Phase II
author: Indus Academy
excerpt: The quant section rewards speed over depth. Here is the question-order strategy our top scorers actually use.
body:
Every year, students walk into the Sukkur IBA Phase II test knowing the math but running out of time. The fix isn't more practice problems, it's a better order of attack.

Start with number systems and percentages. These are fast, formula-driven, and rarely trick you. Save geometry and data sufficiency for your second pass, they eat minutes if you let them.

In our mock test series, students who reordered their attempts this way gained an average of 6-8 extra correct answers without studying a single new topic.

The other half of the equation is elimination. On a multiple choice test, you don't need to solve, you need to rule out. Practice spotting which two options are obviously wrong before you touch a calculator.

title: MDCAT Biology: The High-Yield Chapters Nobody Tells You About
author: Indus Academy
excerpt: Not all chapters carry equal weight. Here's where to spend your last three weeks.
body:
MDCAT Biology has roughly 22 chapters across FSc Part I and II, but the marks distribution is not even close to uniform.

Support and Movement, Coordination and Control, and Reproduction consistently carry the heaviest weight in past papers. If your revision time is limited, this is where it goes first.

We built our chapter-wise test bank around this exact weighting, so students studying with us aren't guessing which chapters matter, they're following the actual exam history.

title: Aror University Admission Test: What First-Time Applicants Should Know
author: Indus Academy
excerpt: A newer test, fewer past papers, and a pattern that still catches Sukkur students off guard.
body:
Aror University's entry test is younger than IBA's, which means fewer past papers are floating around and more students walk in unprepared for the format.

The test leans heavily on English comprehension and analytical reasoning, with a lighter quantitative load than IBA Phase II. Students coming from an IBA-focused prep background usually do well here with minimal extra work.

We run a short orientation session covering eligibility, test pattern, and merit calculation. Details are posted on our notice board when the next session is scheduled.

title: How to Actually Read Your Mock Test Result (Not Just the Score)
author: Indus Academy
excerpt: The number at the top of your result sheet is the least useful part of it.
body:
Students obsess over the total score and skip the sectional breakdown. That's backwards. Your total tells you where you stand today; the breakdown tells you what to fix before the next test.

Look at accuracy per section, not just marks. A student who attempts 15 English questions at 90% accuracy is in better shape than one who attempts 25 at 55%, even if the raw scores look similar.

Track this across mocks, not within one. One bad section is noise. The same weak section three mocks in a row is your study plan writing itself.

title: Beating the Clock on English Comprehension Passages
author: Indus Academy
excerpt: Read the questions before the passage. It sounds backwards, but it isn't.
body:
Most students read a comprehension passage top to bottom, then hunt for answers. That's the slow way.

Skim the questions first. Now you know what you're looking for, main idea, a specific detail, an inference, before you read a single line of the passage. Your reading becomes targeted instead of general.

This single habit change typically saves 90 seconds to 2 minutes per passage in our timed drills, which adds up to a full extra passage attempted by the end of the section.

title: Why We Wrote Himmat Na Haar for Sindhi Students
author: Indus Academy
excerpt: Test prep is only half the battle. The other half is believing you belong in the room.
body:
Every year we watch capable students underperform not because they don't know the material, but because they've already decided they're not the kind of student who gets into IBA.

Himmat Na Haar was written in Sindhi, in plain language, for students who've never had someone tell them directly: your background does not set your ceiling.

It sits alongside our test prep material for a reason. A student who believes they can improve studies differently than one who doesn't.

title: Preparing for MDCAT and IBA at the Same Time: Is It Worth It?
author: Indus Academy
excerpt: Some students hedge their bets across both tests. Here's when that makes sense and when it backfires.
body:
We get this question every intake: should I prepare for MDCAT and IBA Phase II in parallel?

If your foundation in FSc Biology and Chemistry is already strong, dual prep works, the overlap in analytical and English sections carries over well. If you're starting both from scratch three months out, you'll likely do both at a mediocre level instead of one at a strong one.

Our honest advice: pick a primary target based on where your last board exam scores were strongest, and treat the second test as a backup, not an equal priority.

title: The Last Week Before Test Day: What to Stop Doing
author: Indus Academy
excerpt: New topics, all-nighters, and last-minute mock tests all do more harm than good in the final week.
body:
The week before a major admission test is not for learning new material. It's for consolidating what you already know and protecting your energy for test day.

Stop taking full-length mocks after the halfway point of that final week, they add fatigue without adding much diagnostic value at that stage. Switch to reviewing your error log instead: the specific questions you've gotten wrong across past mocks.

And sleep. Every year, the students who score below their mock average almost always report a bad night's sleep beforehand. This is the most controllable variable you have left.
